attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with three weeks of worsening right foot pain and a non-healing ulcer. He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and takes metformin. He first noticed the ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot two months ago after walking barefoot in his garden.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. His right foot is warm, tender and erythematous with a 2 cm deep ulcer on the plantar surface. Peripheral pulses are palpable. Blood tests reveal a white cell count of 14.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0) and C-reactive protein 156 mg/L (<5). He was initially started on intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate as empiric therapy. Subsequently, magnetic resonance imaging shows changes consistent with osteomyelitis of the underlying metatarsal. Blood cultures grew gram-positive cocci in clusters, which, on further testing, are found to be methicillin-sensitive.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with diabetic foot osteomyelitis, which has developed by contiguous spread from an overlying foot ulcer. Diabetic foot infections are particularly concerning due to impaired wound healing, neuropathy, and potential vascular compromise. The morphology and sensitivity pattern is consistent with methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA).

Intravenous flucloxacillin is the most appropriate treatment. Flucloxacillin has excellent activity against MSSA and good bone penetration. Prolonged intravenous therapy is required for osteomyelitis to ensure adequate tissue levels and clinical cure.

Continue intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate would be a suboptimal choice. While it was an appropriate empiric choice for moderate diabetic foot infection, now that MSSA has been identified, changing to the narrower-spectrum flucloxacillin is preferred to reduce selective pressure for resistance.

Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate would be inadequate in this case. Oral antibiotics would not achieve adequate bone concentrations for initial treatment of osteomyelitis.

Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam would be unnecessarily broad-spectrum for a known methicillin-sensitive organism. While it would be active against the organism, using such a broad-spectrum agent when a narrower option is available is not good antimicrobial stewardship. It is appropriate as empiric therapy for severe diabetic foot infection, where an associated systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) is present.

Intravenous vancomycin would be appropriate for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, but is unnecessary when methicillin-sensitive where beta-lactams like flucloxacillin are effective.

In addition to appropriate antibiotic therapy, management should include optimal wound care, pressure offloading, glycemic control, and vascular assessment. Regular monitoring of inflammatory markers and clinical response is essential during treatment.
learningPoints: Diabetic foot osteomyelitis commonly occurs by contiguous spread from overlying soft tissue infection.
Intravenous flucloxacillin is the first-line treatment for methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus osteomyelitis due to excellent bone penetration.
Diabetic foot infections require a thorough assessment of vascular status and wound care in addition to antimicrobial therapy.
references: Lam JC, Stokes W. The Golden Grapes of Wrath - Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia: A Clinical Review. Am J Med. 2023;136(1):19-26. doi:10.1016/j.amjmed.2022.09.017
Antibiotic. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {26 Oct 2024}. https://www.tg.org.au

Lazzarini PA, Raspovic A, Prentice J, et al. Australian evidence-based guidelines for the prevention and management of diabetes-related foot disease: a guideline summary. Med J Aust. 2023;219(10):485-495. doi:10.5694/mja2.52136
